Nighttime Explosion.description: A dark cityscape with a large, fiery explosion in the distance, casting an orange glow over the horizon, suggesting a violent or catastrophic event.

William Burns.description: A man with gray hair, wearing a dark suit and red tie, speaking in a dimly lit room, identified by on-screen text as 'U.S. Ambassador to Russia, 2005–08', whose calm, authoritative voice discusses Ukraine and NATO expansion.

The White House.description: An aerial satellite view of The White House in Washington, D.C., shown with green targeting graphics, signal status indicators, and a digital interface overlay, suggesting surveillance or strategic monitoring.

Thomas Graham.description: A man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking in a dimly lit room, identified by on-screen text as 'Natl. Security Council, 2004–07'. He speaks with a clear, measured voice, recalling the president saying 'we've lost him' and describing Putin's divergent path.

Susan Glasser.description: A woman with short brown hair, wearing a red blazer and a patterned scarf, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit room, identified by on-screen text as 'The New Yorker'. She speaks with a clear, analytical voice, discussing how Putin was allowed to 'get away with it in Georgia' and used the military for political ends, calling it a 'terrible message' and a 'prologue for the Ukraine disaster'.

raw_segments: The video opens with a large, energetic crowd marching in a city square, waving Ukrainian and orange flags, as an unidentified male voice states that democracy can be patterned almost anywhere. This transitions to a chaotic legislative chamber with people scrambling and shouting, while a second male voice declares that Americans respect their courageous choice for liberty and will stand with them. The footage then cuts to Stephen Hadley, National Security Adviser (2005–09), speaking seriously in a dimly lit room, explaining that Putin concluded the US and its intelligence services were installing anti-Russian regimes in neighboring countries. This is intercut with a black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin, his expression intense and focused. An unidentified male voice then states that "Putin had seen enough, he would fight back," as the narrative transitions to a digital satellite interface, zooming from a global view to The Kremlin in Moscow and then Munich, Germany, with targeting graphics, accompanied by a camera shutter sound. An unidentified female news reporter announces that Russian President Vladimir Putin is speaking at an international conference in Germany, as a motorcade with a black Mercedes-Benz and police escort is seen in Munich. Another unidentified male voice confirms Putin would do it in Munich, issuing a warning to America and the world, followed by a brief comment in German about Putin's harsh words from an unidentified German male reporter. Masha Gessen, author of 'The Man Without a Face,' then speaks with urgency, describing Putin at the Munich security conference, not mincing words, and listing all his resentments. The final shot shows an unidentified man in a suit and tie speaking at a podium with 'Friendship' in the background, beginning with "First and foremost."

The video opens with a close-up of Vladimir Putin, wearing a dark suit and striped tie, delivering a serious speech in Russian at the Munich Conference on Security Policy in 2007, as on-screen text confirms. He asserts that the United States has "overstepped its national borders" in economic, political, and humanitarian spheres, imposing its will on other states, and questions, "Who would like this?" The camera cuts to the formally dressed audience, including Strobe Talbott, listening intently. A wide shot reveals the conference hall with banners reading 'Münchner Konferenz für Sicherheitspolitik, 9–11. Februar 2007' and 'Frieden durch Dialog'. As Putin continues to speak in Russian, emphasizing that this situation is "extremely dangerous" and that "no one feels safe anymore," Strobe Talbott, identified as Deputy Secretary of State, 1994–2001, provides a reflective English voiceover, describing Putin's speech as "so searing and blunt," stating, "this was the real guy." The scene then transitions to black-and-white footage of Putin walking through photographers, followed by an interview with Daniel Fried, Assistant Secretary of State, 2005–09, who comments on Putin "accusing the US of making the world unsafe." Finally, Strobe Talbott, in a dimly lit interview setting, further analyzes Putin's address, stating he "was drawing a line and saying, 'We're not going to try anymore. We're just giving up on you, and we're going to make our own world.'" The atmosphere remains formal and tense, reflecting the gravity of the geopolitical discourse.

The video opens with a close-up, black-and-white profile shot of Vladimir Putin, his serious and intense expression emphasizing his stern demeanor, as a male voice states this is "one of Putin's harshest attacks on America in his seven-year term." The scene transitions to an interview with Heather Conley, identified by on-screen text as 'Dep. Asst. Sec. of State, 2001–05', who, in a clear and articulate voice, describes how "after that speech, we saw the first long-range nuclear bombers by Russia" and "the devastating cyber attack on Estonia in 2007." She continues, explaining that "we were starting to see steps taken that represented a Russia assertiveness that we had not seen," and that "we did not necessarily understand that 2007 is the date where he wanted to stop that democratic encroachment." As a low, ominous drone/synth music begins, the footage cuts to a black-and-white shot of Putin inside a car, viewed through the window, his face partially shadowed, reinforcing a sense of mystery and authority. This is followed by a green-tinted satellite view of Munich, Germany, overlaid with digital targeting graphics and a 'Signal Status' bar, suggesting surveillance or strategic monitoring, while a male news reporter announces, "The leaders of NATO begin a two-day summit today." Another female reporter adds, "It's been six years since the establishment of the NATO-Russia Council." The scene then shifts to archival color footage dated 'APRIL 2008', showing George W. Bush exiting a black limousine and walking on a red carpet, waving to the crowd during a formal diplomatic event, as a male reporter states, "But Bush ignored Putin's warning," and discusses how actions "taken by this summit will affect the future of the alliance," "ratcheting up the conflict, encouraging Georgia and Ukraine, two former Soviet Republics, to join NATO," with another male voice concluding that "NATO welcomes the aspirations."

The segment opens with a serious musical underscore as a narrator introduces the topic of Georgia and Ukraine's potential NATO membership. Visually, George W. Bush is seen waving as he exits a vehicle, flanked by Ceremonial Guards in dark blue uniforms with gold trim, opening ornate golden doors. John Beyrle, U.S. Ambassador to Russia (2008–12), then speaks in an interview, his clear, measured voice expressing Russia's profound concern that the Bush administration was moving towards allowing both Georgia and Ukraine into NATO, which he describes as a 'huge red line.' The scene transitions to Vladimir Putin exiting a black limousine on a red carpet, accompanied by security, followed by black-and-white archival images of him in formal settings, including a close-up of his intense gaze. The narrator's authoritative voice explains that Putin rushed to Bucharest to confront Bush. William Taylor, U.S. Ambassador to Ukraine (2006–09), then speaks in an interview, his distinct, slightly gravelly voice recounting an 'amazing moment' where Putin 'shows up early and crashes the dinner' with NATO members, effectively acting as 'the skunk in the garden' and 'challenging them for what they've done.' The narrator continues, stating that in a later meeting, Putin was adamant to Bush that Georgia and especially Ukraine were in 'his sphere of influence.' Taylor then quotes Putin as having leaned over and said, 'Ukraine is not a real country,' emphasizing that Putin had 'no respect for the sovereignty of the Ukrainian people,' concluding with a reflective thought that 'we should have seen it earlier.' The overall tone remains serious and reflective, focusing on high-stakes diplomatic interactions.

The video opens with Steven Pifer, a man in a dark suit and tie, speaking earnestly in a dimly lit room, stating, "Let's clear if we wanted to see it, the direction that President Putin was going." The scene transitions to a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin, shown with a serious, contemplative expression, his face partially obscured, as a male narrator declares, "Putin made it clear he wouldn't accept Bush's plan for Georgia and Ukraine." Julia Ioffe, identified as 'Founding partner, Puck Media', then appears, a woman with shoulder-length brown hair and gold hoop earrings, speaking in a dimly lit setting. She explains, "It basically waved the red flag in front of the bull. It was extremely provocative for Russia. It provoked Russia and made Russia angry." The video cuts to a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Putin’s intense eyes, while the narrator continues, "Just four months later, Putin attacked Georgia." This is followed by chaotic footage of military tanks moving through a war-torn urban environment with buildings ablaze and smoke rising, accompanied by the urgent voice of a news reporter describing "Russian troops backed by tanks and armored vehicles..." Steven Pifer, now identified as U.S. Ambassador to Ukraine (1998–2000), speaks again with a serious expression, stating, "The Russians were prepared and perhaps even eager for a conflict with Georgia. There appeared to be little to stop the troops in their advance," and continuing over a shaky, handheld shot of a man running through rubble, "And part of that was motivated by we're going to show the Georgians that getting too close to NATO has a price." The human cost of war is highlighted by a scene of a person lying injured on the ground amidst debris, as an urgent news report mentions "Russian planes again bombing Georgian..." The final frames depict a soldier in camouflage moving cautiously through a destroyed building, reinforcing the ongoing conflict and destruction, while the narrator adds, "Putin seized and held nearly 5,000 square miles of Georgian territory." An urgent news report further details, "...another town is burning, more civilians have been killed," and another male voice offers analysis, "From Putin's point of view, this wasn't just about Georgia," amidst sounds of conflict.

The segment opens with a woman in a black blouse sitting outdoors, gesturing as she speaks, with a tree and metal fence in the background. This transitions to William Burns, identified by on-screen text as 'U.S. Ambassador to Russia, 2005–08,' speaking in a dimly lit room, his calm, authoritative voice discussing Ukraine and "what he saw to be the West's interest and... eventual expansion of NATO, potentially to include Ukraine and Georgia." The scene then shifts to a dramatic nighttime explosion over a darkened cityscape, followed by a close-up of a bright, chaotic fire. As a narrator's voice describes Georgian soldiers asking, "Where is the United States?", the visuals transition to a vibrant fireworks display over a large stadium, with the Olympic rings illuminated atop a tower, accompanied by the distinct sounds of fireworks and crowd cheering. The narrator, likely Peter Baker, identified as 'Author, Days of Fire,' explains that "As the war broke out, Vladimir Putin and George W. Bush happened to be at the Olympics in Beijing." Black-and-white archival footage shows Vladimir Putin in a dark suit, interacting with others, including a man in a white shirt and tie, in what appears to be a tense conversation. The narrator continues, detailing how "Bush finds himself at the Olympic City sitting in the same row as Putin," moving others aside to speak with him, while being aware of the cameras. The camera focuses on Putin’s intense expression as he listens and speaks, emphasizing his stern demeanor. Peter Baker then appears in a formal interview setting, continuing to narrate how Bush was "telling Putin, 'What are you doing here?'" and that "Putin is basically telling him to butt out." The black-and-white footage returns, showing Putin in close proximity to another man, their intense, focused expressions captured. The segment concludes with a sustained close-up of Putin’s serious face, his eyes wide, suggesting deep concentration or concern, as ominous, low background music plays, and Peter Baker states that "The President who had looked into Putin's soul had now reached a different conclusion about just who Vladimir Putin..."

The video opens with a black-and-white close-up of George W. Bush, his expression serious and contemplative, as ominous, low-frequency background music begins. The scene transitions to a color interview with Thomas Graham, identified as 'Natl. Security Council, 2004–07', who speaks in a clear, measured voice from a dimly lit room, stating, "I remember the president saying, you know, I don't know how, but we've lost him." He continues, explaining that "Putin was going in a different direction, and there was little that the administration, uh, in President Bush's mind could do to put Putin back on that course." A male narrator then adds, "Nearing the end of his presidency, Bush would leave the increasing conflict with Putin to his successor." The video cuts back to a series of dramatic black-and-white close-ups: first, George W. Bush with a furrowed brow, then Vladimir Putin, half-shrouded in shadow, conveying mystery and authority. Next, Susan Glasser, identified as 'The New Yorker', appears in a color interview, wearing a red blazer, speaking with a composed, analytical demeanor. She asserts that "They let Vladimir Putin arguably get away with it in Georgia," elaborating that "One message that Putin clearly takes from that in hindsight is that he is able to uh use the military as a tool of uh achieving his political ends outside the borders of Russia, and that the West will complain and lecture, but basically will let him get away with it." Glasser concludes this is "a terrible message and I think it is the prologue for the Ukraine disaster." The sequence continues with a black-and-white profile shot of a thoughtful man, likely a political figure, before concluding with a green-tinted satellite view of The White House, overlaid with digital targeting graphics and a 'SIGNAL STATUS' bar, creating a tense, high-stakes atmosphere as the ominous music continues.

event_blocks.summary: A satellite view interface targeting Moscow, Russia, accompanied by news snippets announcing Barack Obama's presidency and impending visit, sets the stage for a strategic encounter, with William Burns noting the looming shadows of the Cold War over the summit. Obama's black presidential limousine navigates a bustling Moscow street, and upon arrival at a grand, ornate meeting room, he formally greets and sits across from Vladimir Putin for a high-stakes diplomatic meeting, observed by aides with notepads. Burns recounts their first meeting at Putin's dacha, describing Obama's initial question eliciting a 45-minute monologue from Putin, highlighting their contrasting personalities. Antony Blinken characterizes Putin as extraordinarily calm and matter-of-fact, even when delivering untruths, speaking them as easily as he breathes. The scene returns to the ornate meeting room, where Obama and Putin engage in conversation, Putin gesturing as he speaks, underscored by dramatic music, emphasizing the formal and tense atmosphere of their diplomatic exchange.

Described by Antony Blinken as 'extraordinarily calm, matter-of-fact,' not getting 'ruffled,' and speaking 'mis-truth after mis-truth' but doing so 'as easily as he breathes.' William Burns also recounts Putin delivering a '45-minute monologue' during their first meeting.

William Burns.description: A man with gray hair, wearing a dark suit and red tie, speaking in a dimly lit room, identified by on-screen text as 'U.S. Ambassador to Russia, 2005–08'. He narrates parts of the segment, recalling the first meeting between Obama and Putin in July 2009 at Putin's dacha, describing them as 'much different personalities,' and noting Putin's '45-minute monologue' in response to Obama's 'initial question.'

He provides a detailed character assessment of Vladimir Putin's communication style, noting his calm demeanor while delivering 'mis-truth after mis-truth' and attempting to misinform 'as easily as he breathes.'

Barack Obama.description: A man with short black hair, wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and blue tie, exiting a black limousine, shaking hands with Vladimir Putin, and seated in an ornate room for a formal meeting. News snippets announce him as the 'First African-American president of the United States' and refer to his upcoming visit to Russia, while William Burns mentions his 'initial question' to Putin during their first meeting.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a digital satellite interface displaying Earth from space, green targeting graphics zooming into Moscow, Russia, indicating a strategic focus, accompanied by news snippets announcing Barack Obama as the 'First African-American president of the United States' and his impending visit to Russia. A narrator, later identified as William Burns, U.S. Ambassador to Russia (2005–08), states that 'less than a year later, Vladimir Putin would meet newly elected Barack Obama face-to-face,' with news voices adding that Obama has 'a big meeting ahead,' 'sizing up his third American president.' Burns continues, noting 'the shadows of the Cold War will loom over his summit.' The scene transitions to a bustling Moscow street where a black presidential limousine, adorned with American and Russian flags, drives through traffic. Obama exits the vehicle, greeted by a man in a suit, and proceeds into a grand, ornate room with classical decor. He shakes hands with Vladimir Putin, both in dark suits, and they sit across from each other for a formal meeting, with aides and notepads nearby. Burns, speaking from a dimly lit room, recalls their 'first meeting in July of 2009 at Putin's dacha, just outside Moscow,' describing them as 'much different personalities' and recounting how Obama's 'initial question, about 10 seconds, led to a 45-minute monologue by Putin.' Antony Blinken, V.P. Biden’s National Security Adviser (2009–13), also speaking from a dimly lit room, provides commentary on Putin's communication style, stating he is 'extraordinarily calm, matter-of-fact,' doesn't 'get ruffled,' and 'may say hard things, but he says them in a very calm, almost matter-of-fact way.' Blinken further characterizes Putin as someone who 'simply speaks mis-truth after mis-truth and tries to misinform, but he does it as easily as he breathes.' The video returns to Obama and Putin seated in the ornate meeting room, engaged in conversation, with Putin gesturing as he speaks, while aides observe and take notes, all set against a backdrop of dramatic, underlying music, emphasizing the formal and tense atmosphere of high-stakes diplomacy.

event_blocks.summary: Eugene Robinson of The Washington Post suggested Vladimir Putin's surprise at Barack Obama's election stemmed from racism, believing Obama was not his equal, a sentiment echoed as Putin listened intently and gestured in a luxurious room, with an aide noting the exchange while Obama departed in a black presidential limousine. Satellite imagery then shifted focus from Moscow to the White House, accompanied by news anchors discussing Obama's less warm relationship with Putin and a perceived 'clash of egos,' noting Putin's dominance and Obama's decision to delegate Russia issues to Joe Biden, as depicted in archival footage of Obama and Biden in the Oval Office. Evan Osnos, author of 'Joe Biden,' began to explain Biden's perspective on his vice-presidential role. A narrator introduced Biden as someone who had been discussing Russian issues since 1973, shaped by the Cold War like Putin, and who viewed authoritarian leaders as 'thugs,' a sentiment reinforced by his own passionate Senate audio declaring distrust of the Soviets. Tomichah Tillemann, a former Biden adviser, elaborated on this paradigm, while Biden's Senate audio cautioned against optimism regarding Putin. The scene then shifted to a wide aerial shot of Moscow and the Kremlin, leading to a formal meeting room where Biden would later travel to Moscow to meet Putin. In a subsequent meeting, Biden and Putin stood formally before their flags, but the interaction turned contentious, with Putin described as 'argumentative' and Biden 'wary,' a 'seminal' moment Biden later described in his autobiography 'Promise Me, Dad' as Putin being 'ice-cold calm throughout, but argumentative from the start to finish.' Antony Blinken, present at the meeting, stated Biden's message was to 'speak clearly' and 'speak directly,' not to 'kid each other,' as Biden's voice concluded, 'I'm looking into your...'

His voice is heard reading from his book, describing the meeting as "long and contentious" and Putin as "ice-cold calm throughout, but argumentative from the start to finish," and later saying "I'm looking into your...".

raw_segments: The segment opens with Barack Obama, in a dark suit and blue tie, seated with hands clasped in an ornate gilded armchair, facing Vladimir Putin, also in a dark suit and blue tie, who listens intently and occasionally gestures within a luxurious room featuring a decorative fireplace and white flowers. A male voice, identified as Eugene Robinson of The Washington Post, begins to speak, stating that Putin "did not respect him" and was "surprised that a black man would be elected president of the United States," suggesting an "element of flat out racism" and that Putin "thought Obama was not his equal" and "did not deserve to be treated as his equal." As Robinson speaks, the scene cuts to him in a dimly lit setting, then returns to Putin, who shifts his gaze and posture during the conversation, with a wider shot revealing an aide taking notes nearby. The visual transitions to an outdoor setting where Putin waves as Obama departs in a black presidential limousine. A green-tinted satellite view interface then zooms from Moscow to The White House, displaying targeting graphics and signal status bars, accompanied by a female news anchor's voice noting the relationship was "not quite as warm" and a male news anchor adding that a new president had "another way to deal with the problem of Vladimir Putin." The female anchor continues, stating Putin "gave him an hour-long lecture" and that Obama "likes to be lectured," calling it a "real clash of the egos," and concluding that Obama "has gotten to Moscow and Putin is not buying it, and Putin is still in charge." The male news anchor then mentions giving the "Putin file" to his vice president, Joe Biden, as a black-and-white archival clip shows Obama and Biden in the Oval Office, with Biden gesturing. Finally, Evan Osnos, author of 'Joe Biden,' appears in a dimly lit room, speaking directly to the camera, stating that Biden "sometimes joked that his job as vice president..."

The minute opens with a man in a suit and light blue tie speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, his voice a male narrator explaining that the goal was "to do the things that Obama didn't want to do and that does include the subject of Russia," accompanied by a low, suspenseful background music track. The scene transitions to black-and-white archival footage showing Joe Biden, a man with white hair, in a formal setting with Barack Obama, gesturing as he speaks, while the narrator introduces him as someone "who's been talking about Russian issues since 1973." A close-up of Biden then shows him in a contemplative pose, hand near his mouth, as the narrator states, "And they said, 'Alright, Joe Biden, this is your issue.'" The video then cuts to a grainy, vintage clip of a younger Joe Biden, identified by on-screen text as 'SENATOR JOSEPH BIDEN SALT Supporter,' speaking earnestly, while a new, deeper male voice describes him as "A senator over four decades and chair of the Foreign Relations Committee," continuing that "Biden, like Putin, had been shaped by the Cold War." The scene shows the younger Biden, with on-screen text 'Senate audio,' as his own distinct, passionate voice from a Senate hearing declares, "for myself, I don't trust the Soviets. I don't trust them to do anything short of what they can get away with." The video then cuts to a modern interview with Tomichah Tillemann, identified as 'Sen. Biden adviser, 2005–09,' speaking in a well-lit studio, explaining in his clear voice that "The paradigm through which he saw Putin was similar to the paradigm through which he viewed the other authoritarian leaders," and that "He saw them as thugs." The narrative continues with more black-and-white archival footage of Biden speaking at a Senate hearing, labeled 'Senate audio,' showing him animated and passionate, as his voice from that hearing cautions, "I'd caution against being excessively optimistic about Mr. Putin and his intentions." The scene changes dramatically to a wide aerial shot of Moscow, showcasing the Kremlin and Red Square with its iconic star-topped spire, as the suspenseful background music swells. The final scene shows a formal meeting room with American and Russian flags, where two men in suits walk toward a long table, preparing for a diplomatic discussion, while the deeper male narrator concludes, "Now as Vice President, Biden traveled to Moscow to meet with Putin," accompanied by a faint sound of an airplane or jet engine, underscoring the theme of international relations and travel.

The minute opens with an eerie, low-frequency background music as a color shot shows Joe Biden and Vladimir Putin standing formally side-by-side in front of their national flags, smiling. A calm male narrator states, "The two men smiled for the cameras. But later, things were different." The scene transitions to black-and-white archival footage of Putin and Biden seated at a conference table. Putin, appearing calm and argumentative, is heard speaking briefly in Russian, while the narrator adds, "Putin argumentative." Biden listens intently, his hand near his mouth in a contemplative gesture, with the narrator noting, "Biden wary." A close-up emphasizes Biden’s serious expression. The video then shifts to a modern interview setting with an unidentified man in a suit and glasses, whose voice describes the meeting as "seminal" and a "marker" between the two men. The footage returns to the black-and-white meeting, showing Biden gesturing as he speaks. The original narrator returns, stating, "Biden would write about the moment in his autobiography." A text overlay from Joe Biden’s book, 'Promise Me, Dad,' appears, highlighting the contentious nature of the meeting, as Biden's own voice reads from his book, describing Putin as "ice-cold calm throughout, but argumentative from the start to finish." The scene transitions to a modern interview with Antony Blinken, identified by on-screen text as 'V.P. Biden Natl. Security Adviser, 2009–13,' speaking in a dimly lit room with an American flag in the background. Blinken's voice explains, "Well, I was there with with the then Vice President Biden. Uh, the message that that he's sending is, um, let's not uh, we're not here to kid each other. We need to speak clearly, we need to speak directly." The video concludes with another black-and-white close-up of Biden, his hand near his face, as his voice is heard saying, "I'm looking into your..." before the segment ends, with the background music continuing throughout.

event_blocks.summary: Joe Biden, appearing emotional and reflective in a black-and-white close-up, narrates a past encounter, recalling telling Vladimir Putin, "I don't think you have a soul." Putin, also in a black-and-white close-up with a stern, intense gaze, responded by smiling and stating, "We understand each other, and we did." Antony Blinken, appearing in color in a dimly lit room, recounted his surprise upon hearing Biden's story, initially thinking it sounded like a movie line, but Biden affirmed its veracity, describing Putin as "absolutely positively, that's who this guy is." Amidst somber, dramatic background music, a narrator announced, "And Biden wasn't done." The scene shifted to a formal event at Moscow State University, where Joe Biden, positioned between American and Russian flags with a 'Building on the Reset' backdrop, addressed an audience, urging, "The Russian people want to live in a country that fights corruption... Don't compromise on the basic elements of democracy." A black-and-white close-up of Putin, maintaining his intense and contemplative expression, was shown as the music continued. The segment concluded with a man in a dark suit and glasses speaking to the camera, interpreting Putin's reaction to an American Vice President addressing students and encouraging democracy as a perceived threat.

event_blocks.summary: The segment begins with a black-and-white close-up of a stern Vladimir Putin as a narrator describes actions "aggravating and offensive" to him, transitioning to aerial shots of a massive protest where dense crowds wave flags and signs, including one with 'RUSSIA PUTIN' crossed out and another demanding 'ЧЕСТНЫЕ ВЫБОРЫ'. The crowd chants "Putin!" as Yevgenia Albats describes thousands shouting "down with Putin," expressing Putin's disbelief. Black-and-white close-ups show demonstrators shouting and raising fists, one holding a sign reading 'ТАКИЕ «ВЫБОРЫ» — ПЛЕВОК В НАРОД!', while the narrator states Putin was "thrown by the protest" and points the finger "at us." Chaotic nighttime footage shows police in riot gear arresting protesters, as a reporter notes "Russian police have arrested about 200 people," with PBS and FRONTLINE logos visible. The scene shifts to chaotic street footage of police in riot gear arresting individuals near a 'ПОЛИЦИЯ' bus, accompanied by suspenseful music and a voice describing a "deliberate crackdown." On-screen text details escalating state actions like searches, arrests, and persecution against opposition leaders and in mass media. Gennady Gudkov describes these "targeted repressions" affecting potentially thousands. A montage of newspaper clippings and news headlines highlights Putin's opponents fleeing or dying mysteriously, specifically mentioning Vladimir Kara-Murza's near-fatal poisonings. Vladimir Kara-Murza, in a dimly lit setting, speaks of a "very high mortality rate" among those who "crossed the path of Vladimir Putin's Kremlin," including journalists and anti-corruption campaigners, as the FRONTLINE logo appears.

event_blocks.summary: A man with a receding hairline and beard, speaking in a dimly lit room, discussed 'opposition activists' and the deaths of 'many people,' some in 'strange and unexplained deaths,' others in 'straight out assassinations,' as an electronic hum accompanied a transition to a green-tinted satellite view interface. This interface zoomed from an aerial shot of The Kremlin, complete with targeting graphics and a 'SIGNAL STATUS' bar, then panned to a map highlighting Russia and Ukraine, with Crimea marked. A narrator stated that 'Putin extended his offensive beyond Russia,' while female voices reported 'Ukraine is stuck very much in the middle' and 'Russian troops arrived in the Crimea region,' with the narrator adding the campaign 'began with the arrival of unmarked Russian soldiers.' Footage then showed soldiers in green camouflage uniforms, helmets, and balaclavas, carrying rifles and equipment, marching through urban and rural environments in 2014. Steven Pifer explained that 'They moved in with what the Ukrainians called little green men,' who were 'professional military' in 'Russian-style combat uniforms but no insignia.' As these soldiers took defensive positions, female voices reported 'Russian forces in the thousands seizing territory.' William Taylor described the invasion, sending 'Russian soldiers without insignia into a neighboring country, into Crimea,' as a 'shock' and unprecedented since World War II, before a male voice concluded, mentioning 'occupying sovereign territory.' The segment continued with a close-up of a soldier in full camouflage gear holding a rifle, as a male voice stated, "He seized Crimea and said it was now part of Russia," followed by a Russian voice and another male speaker adding, "And he didn't stop there." The scene shifted to a field where a large artillery piece fired, as the male speaker continued, "He opened another front in Ukraine's east." Soldiers ran through a dusty, war-torn landscape, while a different male voice explained, "He used another form of deception, which was to send in irregular forces, mercenaries who had been recruited," later claiming, "Putin claimed there are no Russian forces in this war," before concluding, "But in fact, he was organizing the whole thing behind the scenes." A stark black-and-white sequence showed the aftermath of conflict: a damaged apartment building with rubble, civilians navigating wreckage, and a distressed woman before a bullet-riddled wall. During this, a female voice remarked that the "Ukraine crisis looks pretty similar to World War II," followed by another stating, "We are seeing a very gross violation of international law." A male voice described it as "just the start of what would become an unrelenting focus on finally taking Ukraine," while a third female voice emphasized, "The humanitarian crisis in Ukraine is getting worse and worse," and another male voice added that "frightened residents have been warned to stay indoors." Eugene Robinson explained, "He felt emboldened, I felt he had been able to make his move in Georgia and nobody tried to do anything about it." The final shot was a black-and-white image of bodies covered in tarps, with armed soldiers and civilians in the background, as a female voice revealed that "an average of 36 people a day were killed in the conflict," before a final male voice mentioned "in Ukraine, he..." The visual style alternated between color and black-and-white, set against a serious background music track.

event_blocks.summary: Amidst the somber imagery of a war-torn cemetery and a young boy facing a bullet-riddled wall, the narrative unfolds with a satellite view transitioning from Ukraine to The White House, overlaid with targeting graphics, as President Obama grappled with responding to Vladimir Putin's actions. Jake Sullivan questioned how to deter behavior when the aggressor denies involvement, while archival footage showed Obama confronting Putin about forces in Ukraine, with Tony Blinken present during those calls, and Putin denying their presence. Vice President Biden urged a tough response, with Tomichah Tillemann noting Biden understood Putin as a bully, and John Brennan suggesting bullies need their 'nose bloodied.' Despite Pentagon and State Department officials, alongside Biden, advocating for arming Ukraine with Javelin missiles, Susan Glasser highlighted that Obama ultimately refused, opting for sanctions instead of weapons, a decision Evan Osnos explained stemmed from Obama not prioritizing Russia and Ukraine, leading Timothy Snyder to warn that Putin learned from Ukraine that invasion could succeed with minimal reaction.
